title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a college tutor called Ben talking about a forest study camp for students. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a college tutor called Ben talking about a forest study camp for students. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A college tutor called Ben describes a forest study camp for students.
extracts.audio_script: Hi everyone. I’m Ben, one of the tutors accompanying this year’s visit to Pinewood Forest Study Camp. You may have seen pictures of cabins among tall trees and imagined the camp was created for holidaymakers. In fact, the central building was originally a [9]research station[/9], used by scientists examining the effects of weather on woodland. It was later extended to provide accommodation for visiting student groups. Our trip will last from Monday until Friday, and the work you do there contributes to your environmental studies project.||The journey is straightforward. We considered hiring a coach directly from college, but this would be expensive for a small group. We will travel most of the way by train, then the camp staff will collect us at the [10]village station[/10] in their minibus. You need not bring camping equipment because we sleep indoors. Each bedroom has four beds and basic storage. Bed linen is supplied, but students should remember a [11]towel[/11], since the camp does not provide these and there is nowhere nearby to buy one easily.||Our first afternoon will introduce the forest. Do not expect to start collecting scientific information immediately. The wardens will lead us on a route designed to improve [12]navigation skills[/12], showing how to use landmarks and a simple map safely. During the next few days, we will complete real monitoring activities. Previous groups checked the number of small mammals in particular areas; our task this year is to measure the growth of [13]young trees[/13], recording height and any signs of damage. This information will help staff decide where new protection is needed.||Outdoor work can be tiring, particularly when it rains. Boots and waterproof coats are essential, and students should bring a notebook in a waterproof cover. The item used by each pair to take measurements will be a [14]measuring tape[/14], which the centre lends to us, along with maps and identification guides. We return to the main building in the late afternoon. There is limited phone signal in parts of the forest, although the dining area has access to [15]wireless internet[/15], mainly so groups can upload results and contact home when necessary. Please do not expect to stream films every night.||One evening will be free, and another will include an optional talk. Last year students heard about woodland birds. This year the visiting expert will speak about [16]forest fires[/16], explaining how dry weather changes the risks in British forests. Before returning home, every group must create a [17]display board[/17] using photographs and observations from the trip; these boards will be shown at college open evening. The trip fee includes transport, food and accommodation. To help reduce the price next year, our class is holding a [18]cake sale[/18] in the college entrance hall next month. Please sign the form today if you can help either by baking or selling. I’m now ready to answer your questions about the visit.

dialogue.speaker: ben
dialogue.text: Hi everyone. I’m Ben, one of the tutors accompanying this year’s visit to Pinewood Forest Study Camp. You may have seen pictures of cabins among tall trees and imagined the camp was created for holidaymakers. In fact, the central building was originally a research station, used by scientists examining the effects of weather on woodland. It was later extended to provide accommodation for visiting student groups. Our trip will last from Monday until Friday, and the work you do there contributes to your environmental studies project.||The journey is straightforward. We considered hiring a coach directly from college, but this would be expensive for a small group. We will travel most of the way by train, then the camp staff will collect us at the village station in their minibus. You need not bring camping equipment because we sleep indoors. Each bedroom has four beds and basic storage. Bed linen is supplied, but students should remember a towel, since the camp does not provide these and there is nowhere nearby to buy one easily.||Our first afternoon will introduce the forest. Do not expect to start collecting scientific information immediately. The wardens will lead us on a route designed to improve navigation skills, showing how to use landmarks and a simple map safely. During the next few days, we will complete real monitoring activities. Previous groups checked the number of small mammals in particular areas; our task this year is to measure the growth of young trees, recording height and any signs of damage. This information will help staff decide where new protection is needed.||Outdoor work can be tiring, particularly when it rains. Boots and waterproof coats are essential, and students should bring a notebook in a waterproof cover. The item used by each pair to take measurements will be a measuring tape, which the centre lends to us, along with maps and identification guides. We return to the main building in the late afternoon. There is limited phone signal in parts of the forest, although the dining area has access to wireless internet, mainly so groups can upload results and contact home when necessary. Please do not expect to stream films every night.||One evening will be free, and another will include an optional talk. Last year students heard about woodland birds. This year the visiting expert will speak about forest fires, explaining how dry weather changes the risks in British forests. Before returning home, every group must create a display board using photographs and observations from the trip; these boards will be shown at college open evening. The trip fee includes transport, food and accommodation. To help reduce the price next year, our class is holding a cake sale in the college entrance hall next month. Please sign the form today if you can help either by baking or selling. I’m now ready to answer your questions about the visit.
